nodes.label: AI as tool for lesson planning
nodes.grounding_span: quickly create a basic structure for a class lesson plan that I then fill in with my own exercises

nodes.label: AI as professional polish tool
nodes.label_negative: AI for creative or technical dance advice
nodes.grounding_span: use AI to check the grammar/spelling/flow of things I need to write

nodes.label: AI trustworthiness and verification
nodes.grounding_span: I'm really hesitant to trust the AI to develop progressions of exercises

nodes.label: AI as writing assistant
nodes.label_negative: AI as replacement for human teaching
nodes.grounding_span: I see it mostly as a helper for writing/editing/organizing

nodes.label: Professional autonomy
nodes.grounding_span: I really didn't like that (it doesn't know my students!)

nodes.label: Student well-being and mental health
nodes.grounding_span: this could be really damaging to students

nodes.label: Efficiency and cost reduction
nodes.grounding_span: just helps me stay organized in the classroom

nodes.label: Satisfaction with AI for design tasks
nodes.grounding_span: The basic structure is usually ok

nodes.label: Distrust of AI reliability
nodes.grounding_span: I'm really hesitant to trust the AI to develop progressions of exercises

nodes.label: Reluctance to use AI for writing
nodes.grounding_span: the text was overly flowery and it added details that weren't in the notes

nodes.label: Optimism about expanding AI use in lesson planning
nodes.grounding_span: I could see myself using it more with my lesson planning/curriculum design

nodes.label: Iterative refinement with limited back-and-forth
nodes.grounding_span: I tend to go back and forth a few rounds to perfect it

nodes.label: Domain-based boundary setting
nodes.grounding_span: I draw a clear line between structural tasks versus the expertise-based content
